title: reverse engineering retro games
summary: A few thoughts on rom-hacking, retro games, and what it means to me.

sections.heading: Gaming and Tech
sections.body: I feel that many engineers have a similar story that involves them enjoying video games from an early age, learning about how horrible working as a game developer at a big company or for an indie company can (possibly) be and then deciding maybe it just isnt worth it.
Growing up in the 2000's many of my peers got to experience the DS/Wii-era games, lots of Nintendo and by extension lots of Mario and Pokémon.

This era was arguably the peak of the mainline Pokémon games and included many beloved by fans such as HeartGold & SoulSilver, Platinum (Diamond & Pearl), as well as my favorites Black & White (including the sequels). While many of the modern games take advantage of high end graphics and much more powerful compute, there is something missing that can only be found in the titles of thier predecessors.

sections.heading: Enter: ROM Hacking
sections.body: A ROM (Read-Only-Memory) is essentially the image of some game from a disc or cartridge. You can imagine something like `Pokemon Emerald.gba` or `New Super Mario Bros.nds`. ROMS contain both game data and executable code; the distinction between these two are cruicial for understanding ROM hacking.

Most game are organized like:
`Game` 
├── `Data` 
└── `Logic`

Data includes stuff like levels, sprites, music. Logic includes stuff like leveling systems, transformation mechanics, rendering systems.

The simplest form of ROM hacking is editing existing game data. Many tools have been created to edit such data and many successful project are data-driven projects. The workflow mainly looks like:

`Open tool`
↓
`Edit data`
↓
`Save ROM`
↓
`Test`

sections.caption: mario sprites in a pokemon game!

sections.heading: From Data Editing to Reverse Engineering
sections.body: At some point just modifying data wasn't enough, as ROM-Hackers became more ambitious, many decided that if desired features didn't exist in the orginal game, we would have to modify the executable code. The most apparent challenge was determining how to edit some resulting executable without the source code:

The solution is to use a disassembler. This enables executable code to be translated back into assembly (for many GBA and DS games this involves [ARM architectures](https://simple.wikipedia.org/wiki/ARM_architecture)). 

This opens the door to a variety of possibilities:

sections.heading: Detective Work > Programming
sections.body:
Reverse engineering becomes a lot more closer to detective work than programming. Imagine some developer trying to figure out where damage calculation is preformed. There is no source code. There are no function names.

Instead the game contains something roughly like:

`0801234:`
 
`LDR R0, [R4,#0x24]`
 
`CMP R0,#0` 

`BEQ ...`

They'd have to determine what `R0` contains, what structure `R4` points to and why offset `0x24` is being read.

Overtime this unknown fucntion becomes known as a damage function and part of some larger battle damage calculator system. But this isn't even the larger issue.

sections.heading: Where are we?
sections.body: A key advantage many Game-Boy Advance (GBA) titles have over DS titles is that communities have completed reverse engineered some games such that they've reconstructed source code that compiles back into identical ROMS. That means instead of working with assembly they can work with code that looks like:

Which is way easier to work with. This has hugely accelarates the development of additional features added to games with fan made ROM-hack titles like [Radical Red](https://www.gamebrew.org/wiki/Pokemon_Radical_Red_GBA) being arguably the pinnacle of the data/logic modifications to Gen 3 pokemon games.

The following GIF shows Garbador, a Pokémon that exists only [8 years after](https://bulbapedia.bulbagarden.net/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon_Black_and_White_Versions) the orignal release of Ruby & Sapphire (base game) using an [in-game mechanic](https://bulbapedia.bulbagarden.net/wiki/Mega_Evolution) that would debut another [3 years after](https://bulbapedia.bulbagarden.net/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon_X_and_Y) that. Moreover, even in the recent iterations, Garbador doesn't even have access to this mechanic in official games!

The DS ecosystem is still catching up and using emulators and debuggers that get hooked into by decompilers like [IDA](https://hex-rays.com/) or [Ghidra](https://github.com/nationalsecurityagency/ghidra) to analyze ARM assembly. See below, IDA hooking into a breakpoint to see ARM code executed when [Hidden Machines (HMs)](https://bulbapedia.bulbagarden.net/wiki/HM) are used.

This creates an interesting problem space where instead of most of the technical problems being framed as "how", (how do I implement this feature, how do I create this workflow) they become framed as "where". Where do I fit this feature and this idea inside an engine that was never designed for it?

sections.heading: Final Thoughts
sections.body:
What attracts me to ROM hacking isn't necessarily the goal of creating the next big fan game or turning game development into a career.

Like many, I grew up playing these games. Pokémon, Mario, and countless other DS and GBA titles were a huge part of my childhood. Over time, I learned more about software engineering and became less interested in the idea of working at a large game studio. Modern game development is its own industry with its own challenges, tools, and pressures.

ROM hacking feels different.

It sits at an intersection of several things I've become curious about: reverse engineering, low-level systems, and software design. Nostalgia is an honorable mention. Every feature added to an old game raises questions that are both technical and historical. How did the original developers solve this problem? Why was the engine designed this way? What limitations were they working under?

The interesting part is that the challenge is rarely just implementing an idea. More often, it's understanding something that already exists. Before you can add a new mechanic, you first have to uncover how the old one works.

In that sense, ROM hacking feels less like traditional software development and more like software archaeology. You're exploring systems that were built decades ago, reconstructing the decisions behind them, and slowly building a mental model of how they fit together.

I don't know if I'll ever release a ROM hack. What I do know is that it gives me an excuse to learn about topics that I find genuinely fascinating: emulation, game engines, and old architectures. All through the lens of games that meant something to me growing up.

And for now, that's more than enough reason to keep digging.
